ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for okra gyudon; closest parent 'beef rice bowl food product' (FOODON_00002809) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for gyudon or okra gyudon; A0CKP (Beef dish) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for gyudon or okra beef rice bowl. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of okra gyudon is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with simmered sliced beef, sliced okra, and dashi sauce, as typically served at a Japanese gyudon chain restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20444), beef short plate simmered (Foundation Foods), okra cooked (SR Legacy #11279), and soy-dashi sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for okra gyudon. Values estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 55% steamed white rice, 25% simmered sliced beef (short plate / chuck), 10% cooked okra, 10% dashi-soy sauce. Per-100g values derived from weighted blend. This is a medium-confidence estimate; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB/USDA branded entry for Sukiya gyudon would supersede these values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled, Japanese-style) GI ~54; gyudon estimated from rice-dominant mixed dish analogy
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for okra gyudon. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being Japanese-style boiled white rice (GI 54, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021). The beef, okra, and dashi sauce components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates and are expected to modestly lower the effective GI of the mixed dish relative to plain rice. GI 54 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate for this dish. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 70 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 37.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and dashi broth
hypertension.guidance: Estimated sodium of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (≈1240 mg per 400 g serving) is a significant contributor to daily sodium intake; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from white rice with moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 400 g serving delivers approximately 70 g of carbohydrate with an estimated glycemic load of 37.8, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion, substitute with brown rice if available, and note that okra's soluble fibre (mucilage) may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and moderate sodium from dashi-soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides an estimated 1.4 g saturated fat per 100 g and approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and choose lean beef preparations to keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Okra Gyudon with Dashi Sauce (オクラ牛丼 だしソースがけ) is a Japanese beef rice bowl dish consisting of a base of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice topped with thinly sliced, simmered beef and sliced okra, finished with a light, tangy dashi-based sauce. Gyudon (牛丼, literally 'beef bowl') is a staple of Japanese fast-casual dining, popularised by chains such as Yoshinoya and Sukiya. In this variant, sliced okra is added as a topping, contributing a characteristic viscous texture due to its soluble fibre content (primarily mucilaginous polysaccharides), which the dish description notes pairs well with the dashi sauce to create a refreshing aftertaste. The dashi sauce is typically made from kombu and katsuobushi (bonito flakes) stock, seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, giving the dish an umami-rich, lightly sweet-savoury flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, protein-containing mixed meal dominated by refined carbohydrate from white rice. Per 100 g it provides approximately 132 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 3.8 g fat, and 17.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and dashi, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is high (estimated GL ≈ 38), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Okra contributes soluble fibre and vitamin C. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in okra gyudon?
What is dashi sauce made of?
What does okra taste like in gyudon?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry (HPB, USDA, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS) exists specifically for okra gyudon. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for cooked white rice, simmered beef, cooked okra, and soy-dashi sauce. GI is estimated from the dominant rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods); a direct laboratory analysis or an approved branded entry for Sukiya okra gyudon would supersede these values. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation and is expected to be negligible in this dish. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style boiled white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); the actual GI of the mixed dish may be modestly lower due to protein, fat, and okra soluble fibre effects. 5. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA beef and rice component data. 6. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Mixed', which accurately reflects a complete beef rice bowl meal. 7. Halal status is listed as false because dashi typically contains katsuobushi (bonito/fish); consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 8. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
